stories.headline: Lawyer warns against debate on voting threshold for VP Duterte conviction during pretrial
stories.summary: Election lawyer Romulo Macalintal warned against raising the question of the vote threshold needed to convict Vice President Sara Duterte during the ongoing pretrial stage, saying it would delay proceedings. He stated the Constitution requires a two-thirds vote of all 24 senators and that debating whether absent or detained senators should be excluded is premature. The Senate impeachment trial is scheduled to begin on July 6.
stories.context: VP Duterte was impeached by the House on May 11, 2026, on charges including alleged misuse of confidential funds, bribery, and purported threats against President Marcos and other officials. The threshold question arose because some senators are detained or otherwise absent, prompting proposals to recalculate the two-thirds requirement.
stories.watch_for: The Supreme Court may clarify the voting threshold through a petition filed by Senator Alan Peter Cayetano; the trial is set to open on July 6.

stories.headline: Typhoon Francisco threatens northern Philippines with heavy monsoon rains and coastal hazards
stories.summary: Typhoon Francisco (international name: Mekkhala) intensified over the Philippine Sea on June 21, carrying maximum sustained winds of 120 kph and gusts of 150 kph, PAGASA reported. The typhoon is unlikely to make direct landfall but will enhance the southwest monsoon, bringing heavy rains to Northern Luzon and western portions of Luzon and Visayas starting Monday. Tropical cyclone wind signal number one may be raised in northern and eastern Cagayan, the Babuyan Islands, and Batanes.
stories.watch_for: PAGASA expects Francisco to reach peak intensity by Tuesday, June 23; wind signal number one may be raised in extreme northern Luzon by Monday morning.

stories.headline: Marcos approves additional P3 billion to repatriate and reintegrate Filipinos displaced by Middle East conflict
stories.summary: President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. authorized the release of an additional P3 billion to fund the full repatriation and reintegration of overseas Filipino workers and their dependents displaced by the ongoing Middle East conflict. As of June 17, a total of 10,446 Filipinos had returned home, comprising 8,281 workers, 1,803 dependents, and 362 stranded citizens. The program covers flights, financial assistance, medical assessments, counseling, and livelihood support, with some recipients receiving up to P20,000 in startup aid.

stories.headline: Marcos administration pursues investment package to position Philippines as semiconductor and AI hub
stories.summary: President Marcos is pushing to attract investment that would establish the Philippines as a center for semiconductor manufacturing and artificial intelligence in Asia, according to Executive Secretary Ralph Recto. The government is positioning Pax Silica, a 4,000-acre US-led industrial complex in New Clark City, Capas, Tarlac launched in April 2026, as a key draw, with over 50 companies reportedly expressing interest. Planned facilities include semiconductor fabrication plants, critical minerals processing centers, and hyperscale data centers.
stories.context: The government is also leveraging the Luzon Economic Corridor, a joint US-Japan infrastructure framework, to attract semiconductor and technology investment aligned with global supply chain diversification efforts.

stories.headline: PNP chief orders intensified internal discipline after arrest of two active-duty officers
stories.summary: Philippine National Police chief Gen. Jose Melencio Nartatez Jr. directed the intensification of the PNP's internal cleansing campaign after two active-duty officers were arrested on June 20. One officer from Quezon City faces rape charges, while another from Davao del Norte is charged with two counts of domestic abuse under the Anti-Violence Against Women and Their Children Act. Nartatez said the PNP badge would never shield anyone from the law.

stories.headline: Angat Dam water level falling amid low rainfall; Puerto Galera declares water shortage calamity
stories.summary: PAGASA reported that the Angat Dam's water level is declining due to below-average rainfall, with only 120 millimeters recorded nationwide as of June 20, roughly half the projected monthly amount. The critical monitoring threshold for the dam is 160 meters, below which a water supply deficiency for Metro Manila may occur. Puerto Galera in Oriental Mindoro has separately declared a state of calamity, with 11 of 13 barangays reporting water shortages.
stories.context: The low rainfall is attributed to El Nino conditions. Authorities are exploring alternative water supply sources including Laguna de Bay and treatment facilities in nearby provinces, and water allocation to farmlands is being reduced to conserve supply.

stories.headline: Philippine Marines lead US, South Korean, and coast guard units in joint amphibious exercise in Cavite
stories.summary: The Philippine Marine Corps led Exercise Kamandag 2026 in Ternate, Cavite on June 20, involving the United States Marine Corps, Republic of Korea Marine Corps, the Philippine Coast Guard Special Operations Force, and the PNP Maritime Group. The exercise focused on amphibious raid tactics, rapid deployment, and interoperability among military and law enforcement units in maritime security scenarios. Observers from Canada, Spain, and Thailand also participated.
stories.context: Exercise Kamandag, whose name means Cooperation of the Warriors of the Sea, has evolved from a bilateral US-Philippines drill into a multilateral framework emphasizing interoperability amid evolving challenges in the Indo-Pacific maritime domain.

stories.headline: BSP waives mark-to-market accounting for banks hit by bond yield surge tied to Middle East conflict
stories.summary: The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas announced it will allow banks and quasi-banks to reclassify peso-denominated government securities from their trading portfolios to a held-to-collect category, waiving mark-to-market accounting requirements from April through December 2026. The relief responds to a roughly 200-basis-point surge in bond yields attributed to the Middle East conflict, which has reduced the market value of government securities held by Philippine banks. Institutions using the relief may face restrictions on certain central bank liquidity facilities.

stories.headline: Philippines tops Southeast Asia in budget transparency for second consecutive survey cycle
stories.summary: The Philippines ranked first in Southeast Asia in the 2025 Open Budget Survey, scoring 76 out of 100 on transparency, accountability, and public participation—one point higher than its 2023 result—according to the International Budget Partnership. Indonesia placed second at 70, the only other Southeast Asian nation to achieve a score classified as sufficient. The survey noted a decrease in the detail provided in the enacted budget, particularly regarding revenue and debt data.

stories.headline: US and Iran open ceasefire talks in Switzerland with Strait of Hormuz and nuclear program on the table
stories.summary: US Vice President JD Vance led the first round of negotiations with Iran at Switzerland's Burgenstock resort on June 21, under a memorandum of understanding established a week earlier. The talks aim to formalize an interim deal covering the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and a halt to hostilities on all fronts. Iran's delegation said discussions focused on frozen assets and oil sanctions, and indicated that resolving the conflict in Lebanon was a prerequisite for progress on other issues.
stories.context: Continued Israeli strikes in Lebanon since March have killed over 4,000 people and strained the ceasefire framework. Iran has cited those strikes as an obstacle to implementing the deal, and President Trump issued fresh warnings that Iran would face military action again if its proxies in Lebanon did not stand down.
stories.watch_for: Further negotiating rounds are expected in Switzerland; analysts cited by Al Jazeera warned that continued Israeli operations in Lebanon could collapse the agreement.

stories.headline: Ethiopia's ruling Prosperity Party wins parliamentary majority, securing Abiy Ahmed's continued leadership
stories.summary: Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed's Prosperity Party secured a comfortable parliamentary majority in Ethiopia's June 2026 elections, ensuring his continued leadership, Al Jazeera reported. The campaign focused on economic growth and food security, with the government projecting over 10 percent GDP growth for the year. Voting was not held in the Tigray region due to what authorities described as unfavorable conditions.
stories.context: Abiy won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2018 for ending a border conflict with Eritrea, but human rights organizations say his government has since detained journalists and restricted civil society. Ethiopia faces ongoing ethnic violence in Oromia and Amhara, and analysts warn of renewed tensions in Tigray after a political party there moved in May to reassert control, potentially threatening a 2022 peace deal.

stories.headline: Japan's Toto to invest $495 million in semiconductor materials targeting next-generation AI chip production
stories.summary: Japanese company Toto plans to invest 80 billion yen ($495 million) over five years to expand its semiconductor materials operations and develop products targeting next-generation chips in the 1-nanometer range, Nikkei Asia reported. The investment is driven by surging demand for advanced semiconductor materials fueled by artificial intelligence applications. The move reinforces Japan's position as a key supplier of critical materials to the global chipmaking supply chain.
